entities.description: Legacy electronic text distributed by U-M in 1993 and through OTA. OTA item 1679 identifies itself as replaced by ota:3034.

notes: The U-M identifier and OTA legacy number are treated as identifiers for one legacy encoding family rather than as evidence for two independent textual transcriptions.

bibliographic.citation: Everyman, ed. A. C. Cawley, Old and Middle English Texts (Manchester: Manchester University Press, 1961), xxxviii + 47 pp.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

entities.description: The immediate print source named in the repository XML and OTA replacement. Cawley's edited text occupies 47 pages after xxxviii pages of front matter and includes a facsimile. Evidence reviewed here supports an eclectic text based primarily on the Huntington/Britwell Skot copy rather than Huth alone; the exact 1961 volume itself was not publicly digitized and was not directly inspected.
entities.label: A. C. Cawley's 1961 edition of Everyman
notes: A corrected 1977 reprint with additional bibliography has ISBN 0719001153 and xl + 47 pages; it must not be silently substituted for the exact 1961 carrier named by the XML.
The XML says series number 1, while a Folger record for a 1965 issue says number 3. This manifest does not force one reissue's series numbering onto the 1961 source.

entities.description: A complete scanned Everyman's Library anthology edited by A. C. Cawley. Its undated title page and second-edition preface do not establish an exact issue date; catalogs place the Cawley edition around 1959–1960. The direct Everyman source note states that its text is based on the Huntington Skot copy with readings from the other three print objects then known. It is a collateral Cawley edition, not the immediate 1961 Manchester source, and it predates recovery of the Bandinel Q1 leaves.
entities.label: Cawley, Everyman and Medieval Miracle Plays, second-edition carrier (c. 1959–1960)
notes: The scan literally says ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922,’ an inherited series/publication-history line that the provider misapplies as the date of this undated modern Cawley carrier. The provider's public-domain field is not used for rights analysis.
The anthology modernizes presentation and is useful evidence for Cawley's base-copy statement, but it is not assumed to be textually identical with the 1961 original-spelling Manchester edition.

access.entityLabel: Richard Pynson Q1 Douce fragment, Bodleian Library (USTC 501308)

bibliographic.citation: [Everyman] ([London]: Richard Pynson, c. 1515), surviving Bodleian fragment; USTC 501308, STC 10604, ESTC S114635.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

entities.description: One of two detached physical survivors of the earliest Pynson print state, Q1. The Bodleian Douce fragment contains four leaves of signature C and preserves lines 684–922, with the top six lines of each leaf lost through trimming. The separately modeled Bandinel leaves preserve another portion of the same state. Current USTC assigns STC 10604 and ESTC S114635 but lists only the Bodleian holding; the 2007 METS edition predates recovery of the Bandinel leaves.

holding.notes: The reviewed public USTC record names the Bodleian but leaves its shelfmark blank; ‘Douce Fragment’ is the designation used by METS and EMOTHE. No shelfmark is invented.

entities.label: Richard Pynson Q1 Douce fragment, Bodleian Library (USTC 501308)
notes: The Douce and Bandinel fragments together preserve 325 lines of Q1; they are two physical objects from one print state, not two editions.

physicalDescription.extent: Four leaves, signature C; lines 684–922 survive, with the top six lines of each leaf trimmed away

rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.
rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the c. 1515 fragment
notes: This does not override contractual terms or separate rights in modern photographs and subscription databases.

access.accessMethod: Consult Freeman's peer-reviewed article and EMOTHE's public witness analysis. These describe the fragment but do not provide a verified current object catalog or complete public facsimile.

access.accessMethod: No complete public page-image set or direct object-download route was verified. The last reviewed provenance places the leaves with Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman in 2009; their current holder and location are unknown.
access.entityLabel: Richard Pynson Q1 Bandinel fragment (signatures B3–B4)

bibliographic.citation: [Everyman] ([London]: Richard Pynson, c. 1515 or c. 1518–1519), Bandinel fragment, signatures B3–B4, lines 429–552; Q1 / STC 10604 / USTC state 501308.
bibliographic.title: Everyman

dateStatements.label: current USTC dating of print state
dateStatements.notes: USTC 501308 currently lists only the Bodleian survivor; this date is applied at state level, not as a cataloged holding record for the Bandinel object.

entities.description: A separately preserved two-leaf survivor of the same earliest Pynson state as the Bodleian Douce fragment. The Bandinel leaves are signatures B3–B4 and preserve lines 429–552. Arthur Freeman reported them in 2008 among the recovered Bandinel fragments; they were held by Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman as of 2009, but their current location is unverified. Because their recovery postdates Cawley's 1961 edition, they are collateral Q1 evidence and not a source for Cawley's text.
entities.label: Richard Pynson Q1 Bandinel fragment (signatures B3–B4)
notes: Together with the four Douce leaves, the Bandinel leaves bring Q1's surviving extent to 325 lines.
No current institutional holding, shelfmark, catalog record, or public facsimile was verified.

rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.
rights.component: Historical printed text and typography of the early-sixteenth-century Bandinel fragment
notes: This conclusion concerns the historical printed content only. Current ownership, access conditions, and any rights or terms applying to modern photographs remain unknown.

bibliographic.title: The Somonynge of Everyman

entities.description: A complete Skot printing, formerly at Britwell Court. Current USTC dates it c. 1528 and assigns STC 10606 and ESTC S105555. Cawley's collateral anthology expressly identifies the Huntington Skot copy as its base; the 1961 source and XML readings support it as the principal base for the immediate Cawley lineage as well.
entities.label: John Skot complete Everyman, Huntington Library 14195 (USTC 502061)
notes: The 2007 METS early-edition list assigns this same Huntington/Britwell state a broader c. 1530–1535 date; current USTC gives 1528.

rights.basis: The printing dates to the early sixteenth century, centuries beyond ordinary copyright terms.

bibliographic.pages: 106-page downloadable edition; English Everyman on printed pp. 17–77
bibliographic.title: Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc

entities.description: A modern parallel scholarly edition edited by Clifford Davidson, Martin W. Walsh, and Ton J. Broos. Its English copytext is British Library Huth 32, corrected from the Huntington Skot copy and the two then-known Pynson state representatives, British Library C.21.c.17 and the Douce fragment. Published in 2007, it predates Freeman's report of the separate Bandinel Q1 leaves. It supplies strong scholarly comparison for source direction, early-print census as then known, editorial readings, and the distinction between Huth and Cawley's eclectic text.
entities.label: METS Everyman and Its Dutch Original, Elckerlijc (2007)

rights.basis: METS expressly states that all editions are under copyright and that current digital editions are for individual use only; reprinting requires permission.

notes: METS separately licenses its XML as CC BY-NC-SA 4.0. That XML-specific license is not transferred to the cached PDF or to all edition content.

entities.description: Jesús Tronch Pérez's public critical modernized edition based on Q3, the complete Huntington Skot copy, with a TEI apparatus recording Q1, Q2, Q3, and Q4 readings and modern editorial sources. Its witness analysis incorporates the post-Cawley Bandinel discovery and distinguishes the two physical fragments of Q1.
entities.label: EMOTHE critical modernized edition of The Summoning of Everyman (2019)
notes: The TEI embeds DOI 10.5281/zenodo.3958901, but that DOI returned an unregistered-identifier response during review; the live EMOTHE landing page and exact TEI URL remain authoritative access routes.

next_steps: Obtain or borrow the exact 1961 edition rather than a 1965 issue or corrected 1977 reprint.
Photograph or transcribe its title verso, preface, textual principles, and apparatus within applicable law and provider terms.
Compare diagnostic XML readings with the 1961 text and all four early states, while keeping the post-1961 Bandinel leaves collateral rather than imputing them to Cawley.
notes: The present Huntington-base edge is deliberately supported/medium rather than confirmed/high until the immediate volume is checked.
openQuestions.question: Can the exact 1961 Manchester volume be inspected to verify its preface, base-copy declaration, apparatus, corrections, and differences from Cawley's anthology and later reprints?

next_steps: Check Huntington's current digital catalog by shelfmark 14195 and USTC 502061.
Use the linked EEBO record through an entitled institution if available.
Ask Huntington imaging services whether a complete color reproduction can be ordered and what publication terms apply.
notes: A current Huntington Digital Library search by title and shelfmark did not expose a matching public object. Huntington states that only a fraction of its holdings are digitized and offers imaging or virtual reading-room routes, so absence from public search is not evidence that no images exist.
openQuestions.question: Is a free, complete, directly downloadable whole-object facsimile available for Huntington Library 14195, Cawley's principal Skot base copy?

next_steps: Ask Bodleian Special Collections to resolve the historical ‘Douce Fragment’ designation against current catalog data.
Request a complete-object image quotation and an exact work/surface inventory.
Retain the USTC record and METS designation without inventing a shelfmark.
openQuestions.question: What is the current exact Bodleian shelfmark for the Pynson ‘Douce Fragment,’ and can the complete surviving object be obtained as a public or requestable facsimile?

next_steps: Trace ownership after Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman's reported 2009 holding through provenance scholarship and current institutional catalogs.
Ask Freeman's publisher, relevant rare-book repositories, or the editors who facilitated EMOTHE materials for a current holding and shelfmark.
Do not infer current ownership or image rights from the 2008 discovery article or 2009 EMOTHE provenance note.
openQuestions.question: Where are the Bandinel Q1 leaves now held, and can the complete two-leaf object be accessed or reproduced?

next_steps: Keep each statement scoped to its own carrier and contribution layer.
Do not treat U-M, OTA, or repository licensing as clearance for Cawley's modern editorial choices.
Seek qualified legal advice or rights-holder permission before redistributing a close reproduction of protected editorial matter.
openQuestions.question: How should the current U-M NoC-US assertion, OTA Creative Commons licenses, legacy personal-scholarly-use restriction, and protected Cawley source be reconciled for downstream redistribution?

next_steps: Recheck USTC, ESTC/CERL, holding-library records, and new bibliographical studies during future reviews.
Preserve Freeman/EMOTHE and old METS/STC chronologies as competing scholarly evidence while using current USTC identifiers for discovery.
Keep four print states distinct from five surviving physical objects even if catalogs continue to list only the Douce holding for USTC 501308.
openQuestions.question: Can the c. 1515 USTC date and c. 1518–1519 Freeman/EMOTHE date for Q1 be reconciled, and will revised cataloging represent both physical Q1 fragments while retaining one print-state identity?

next_steps: Compare collation, binding, title verso, and second-edition preface with authenticated 1959 and 1960 cataloged copies.
Continue to label the cached carrier c. 1959–1960 and do not apply its inherited ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922’ line as the date of Cawley's edition.

reviewNotes: The repository XML's exact 1485 creation date is a legacy assertion, not a securely established composition date.

The eighteen DIV1 containers and supplied Scene headings were added by Ian Lancashire; their numbering omits Scene 4 and the first DIV1 contains an inherited ‘French scene’ label. They are encoding navigation, not an intrinsic scene hierarchy.
Current USTC numbering and dating differ from the older STC sequence printed in the 2007 METS introduction. Both are preserved without forcing false agreement.

The cached Greg/Farmer volume reproduces all of British Library Huth 32 and the surviving latter two-thirds of British Library C.21.c.17. It does not reproduce the Huntington/Britwell Skot base copy or the Bodleian Pynson fragment.

The Digital Library of India metadata applies ‘1922’ and public-domain fields to a modern Cawley anthology. The scan does contain the inherited series-history line ‘First published in Everyman's Library 1922,’ but its title page and second-edition prefaces establish a later Cawley carrier. The provider has misapplied the inherited line as this carrier's date, and its public-domain field is not accepted as rights clearance.

Q1 is not a single unique physical survivor: the Douce fragment preserves four leaves, signature C, lines 684–922, while the separately recovered Bandinel fragment preserves two leaves, signatures B3–B4, lines 429–552. Together they preserve 325 lines from one print state.

The Bandinel leaves were reported in 2008, after Cawley's 1961 edition. As of 2009 they were held by Arthur and Janet Ing Freeman; their current location and access status remain unverified, so no Cawley-to-Bandinel collation edge is asserted.

The lineage schema has no dedicated historical-printed-witness entity type. Its nearest class, scholarly_edition, is therefore used for each early print object or fragment; that technical classification does not claim modern critical editing.
Cawley's 1961 editorial text and apparatus are treated as copyright protected. Public-domain and open-license statements for other digital layers do not transfer to that edition.
The complete METS provider PDF is retained byte-for-byte. It renders and extracts all 106 pages, although qpdf reports recoverable object-offset warnings.

lineage.summary: The repository artifact copies a 1993 University of Michigan/Oxford Text Archive encoding identified as U-1350-A and later as ota:1679. Its header explicitly names A. C. Cawley's 1961 Manchester edition as the source and says that prefatory and critical matter was omitted and that Ian Lancashire supplied acts and scenes. Current evidence indicates that Cawley's text is an eclectic edition based primarily on the complete Huntington/Britwell Skot printing, with readings from the four early print states known to him; it is not a transcription of the Huth copy alone. The English work survives in four early printed states and no known manuscript witness, but those states occupy five physical objects because Q1 is divided between the separately preserved Douce and Bandinel fragments. The Bandinel leaves were reported only in 2008 and are collateral evidence, not a source Cawley could have consulted in 1961. A complete 1912 facsimile volume containing Huth 32 and the surviving British Library Q2 fragment, the complete OTA 3034 replacement encoding, complete METS and EMOTHE editions, and a complete but rights-restricted Cawley anthology scan are cached privately for verification. Each historical print object, scholarly edition, encoding, repository file, facsimile, access route, and rights statement remains a distinct layer.

catalogSummary.author: Anonymous English translator-adapter

catalogSummary.region: England; translated and adapted from the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc tradition of the Low Countries
catalogSummary.title: Everyman

catalogingSubject.label: Everyman
catalogingSubject.scope_note: The anonymous Middle English morality commonly called Everyman, treated as one intellectual work. Four early printed states survive in five known physical objects because the earliest Pynson state is divided between the separately preserved Douce and Bandinel fragments. Those print objects, A. C. Cawley's 1961 edition, the U-M/OTA encodings, modern scholarly editions, and facsimiles are transmission layers rather than separate works. The eighteen DIV1 elements and supplied scene headings in the repository XML are editorial navigation imposed by Ian Lancashire, not eighteen constituent works or an authorial scene structure.

contentParts.label: The Somonyng of Everyman

locators.value: 19 and every odd PDF page through 79 (printed pp. 17–77); PDF p. 17 is the Huth title image

notes: The METS edition numbers an added terminal Amen as line 922; the repository/Cawley sequence has 921 numbered verse lines. This is an editorial lineation difference, not evidence of a second work.
contentParts.summary: Complete dramatic verse from the Messenger's prologue, through Death's summons and Everyman's failed and successful searches for companions, to his death, reception by the Angel, and the Doctor's moral epilogue.

dateStatements.display: Late fifteenth or early sixteenth century; exact date unknown
notes: The repository XML and OTA replacement repeat 1485 as a creation date, but the reviewed sources do not establish that exact date. It is therefore retained only as a legacy transmission assertion and not normalized as the work's composition date.

dateStatements.display: Earliest surviving Richard Pynson state: c. 1515 under current USTC dating, or c. 1518–1519 in Freeman/EMOTHE; exact date uncertain
notes: The earliest state (STC 10604 / USTC 501308) survives in two detached physical fragments: four Douce leaves preserving lines 684–922 and two Bandinel leaves preserving lines 429–552, together 325 lines. Current USTC dates the state c. 1515 and lists only the Bodleian copy; Freeman and EMOTHE date it c. 1518–1519 and supply the separately held Bandinel evidence. No singular lineage binding is used for this state-level date.

extent.description: One 921-line dramatic verse work, from the Messenger's prologue through the Doctor's epilogue, plus brief prose title rubric, stage directions, FINIS, and Skot colophon. The repository XML has 18 supplied DIV1 scene containers, 19 HEAD elements, 921 numbered L elements, 211 speeches, 28 page milestones, nine signature milestones, four STAGE elements, and three FOREIGN elements. Those encoding counts do not redefine the work as 18 scenes or as mixed prose and verse.

formStatements.description: A 921-line dramatic verse text with brief prose rubrics, stage directions, and a printer's colophon. Those paratextual prose features do not make the work mixed-form.

genres.label: Morality play
notes: The early descriptive title says the treatise is ‘in maner of a morall playe.’ No performance is recorded before 1901, so the label does not itself prove medieval stage performance.

genres.label: Devotional literature
notes: The early title calls it a treatise, and modern scholarship emphasizes its compatibility with late-medieval reading about dying and the spiritual life.

languageStatements.label: Latin
notes: Three FOREIGN elements encode ‘In manus tuas,’ ‘Commendo spiritum meum,’ and ‘Ite, maledicti, in ignem eternum.’

scope.description: Brief liturgical or biblical phrases in XML lines 886–887 and 915

notes: Cawley's modern editorial work and the historical English work have different rights statuses; see the linked lineage manifest.

No English manuscript witness is known. Four early print states survive in five physical objects: three fragmentary objects (Douce Q1, Bandinel Q1, and British Library Q2) and two complete Skot copies.

The cached Greg/Farmer facsimile supplies direct images of Huth 32 and British Library C.21.c.17, but not the Huntington/Britwell Skot copy that Cawley identifies as his base or either physical fragment of the earliest Pynson state.

next_steps: Retain a broad late-fifteenth/early-sixteenth-century date rather than the XML's unsupported exact 1485.
Review new textual scholarship on English–Dutch direction, rhyme evidence, and the chronology of Elckerlijc prints.
openQuestions.question: When and where was the English translation-adaptation composed, and which lost or surviving Dutch state supplied its exact exemplar?

next_steps: Prefer current USTC/ESTC identifiers while preserving the different Freeman/EMOTHE and 2007 METS chronologies in notes.
Recheck USTC and holding-library catalogs during future manifest review, including whether USTC 501308 can represent both detached Q1 fragments.
openQuestions.question: How should the competing c. 1515 USTC and c. 1518–1519 Freeman/EMOTHE dates for Q1 be resolved, and will future catalog revisions alter the dating, ordering, or STC mapping of the four early English print states?

notes: This identifies the English linguistic and early print context, not a securely documented city or place of composition.
The association belongs to the Middle Dutch source tradition; it does not relocate the anonymous English adaptation.
Current scholarship regards the English work as a translation and adaptation of Elckerlijc, but neither the English translator nor the exact Dutch exemplar is known.
The adaptation role records substantive English reshaping as well as translation; it is not a claim for a second person.

subjects.label: The transience and desertion of worldly attachments

summaries.value: God sends Death to summon Everyman to a final reckoning. Fellowship, Kindred, Cousin, and Goods refuse his journey; Good Deeds is initially too weak to accompany him, but Knowledge leads him through Confession and penance until Good Deeds can rise. Beauty, Strength, Discretion, and Five Wits pledge aid but abandon him at the grave. Only Good Deeds enters with him, while Knowledge remains to hear the angels welcome his soul. A Doctor's epilogue directs hearers to reject pride and prepare their accounts before death. The English work translates and reshapes the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc into an allegorical meditation on mortality, sacramental preparation, charity, and salvation.

This record describes the anonymous work, not a single early print, Cawley's 1961 edited carrier, or the electronic encoding. The repository XML's eighteen DIV1 elements and visible ‘Scene’ headings are supplied modern navigation. Scene 4 is absent, numbering runs from 1 to 19, and the first DIV1 also contains the inherited label ‘French scene’; these encoding artifacts are not used as the work's intrinsic content hierarchy.

notes: Normalized discovery title. The early Skot title pages carry a much longer descriptive title, and the opening messenger names the work The Somonynge of Eueryman.
Catalog spelling normalized from varying early forms; XML line 4 reads ‘The Somonynge of Eueryman.’
A normalized transcription of the descriptive title used for the complete Skot printings; lineation and letterforms differ between carrier and catalog display.

subjects.label: The transience and desertion of worldly attachments

work.summary: God sends Death to summon Everyman to a final reckoning. Fellowship, Kindred, Cousin, and Goods refuse his journey; Good Deeds is initially too weak to accompany him, but Knowledge leads him through Confession and penance until Good Deeds can rise. Beauty, Strength, Discretion, and Five Wits pledge aid but abandon him at the grave. Only Good Deeds enters with him, while Knowledge remains to hear the angels welcome his soul. A Doctor's epilogue directs hearers to reject pride and prepare their accounts before death. The English work translates and reshapes the Middle Dutch Elckerlijc into an allegorical meditation on mortality, sacramental preparation, charity, and salvation.
